1: Rehoboam went to Shechem (šĕḵem) for all Israel had come to Shechem to make him king (lĕhamlik ʾōtô). Shechem, not Jerusalem: this is the politically charged northern assembly city — site of the covenant-renewal under Joshua (Joshua 24:1), and now the site of the kingdom’s fracture. The Chronicler does not explain why Shechem was chosen; the geography itself signals that the northern tribes are holding the coronation on their own terms, in their own territory.
2: Jeroboam son of Nebat was in Egypt (bĕmiṣrayim) where he had fled from King Solomon. When the people sent (wayyišlĕḥû) for him, he came (wayyāḇōʾ). The Hiphil wayyišlĕḥû (they sent) shows the people as active agents summoning Jeroboam, not him returning opportunistically. Egypt as refuge echoes Moses’s flight to Midian and anticipates the later Egyptian exile. The return from Egypt will complete the narrative arc: what comes from Egypt disrupts the Davidic line.
3: ‘Come to us and lighten the yoke (qal mēʿōl ʾāḇîḵā) of your father.’ ʿōl (yoke) is the servitude/oppression metaphor from the Exodus narrative (Leviticus 26:13: YHWH broke the ‘bars of your yoke’). The people’s demand frames Solomon’s labor as Pharaonic: the liberated nation finds itself under a new ʿōl. qal (make light, Hiphil of qālal) = reduce, lessen. The identical formula for oppressive labor appears in Exodus 5:9 where Pharaoh commands ‘let the work be heavy upon the men.’
4: ‘Your father made our yoke heavy (hiḵḇîḏ ʾet-ʿullēnû)’ — kāḇēḏ (Hiphil: to make heavy/burdensome). The root k-b-d also produces kāḇôḏ (glory, weight, honor). To ‘make heavy’ the yoke is linguistically to make it glorious/weighty — a dark irony when the Davidic king’s glory-weight falls on his people as burden. waʿattāh hāqēl = ‘and now lighten it’ (qal imperative of qālal) — the people’s appeal for relief is grammatically an imperative: command, not request.
5: Rehoboam says ‘Come back to me in three days’ (šûḇû ʾēlay ʿôḏ šĕlōšet yāmîm). The three-day interval appears throughout the OT at pivotal moments (Genesis 22:4; Exodus 19:11; Joshua 1:11; Jonah 1:17). The king needs three days to deliberate — but the decision is already structurally predetermined. šûḇû (return, come back) is the repentance verb; its use here for a political return is theologically neutral, but in Chronicles where šûḇ nearly always carries theological weight, the casual use foreshadows the spiritual failure that follows.
6: Rehoboam consulted (wayyiwwāʿaṣ) the elders (zĕqēnîm) who had served his father Solomon while he lived. nāʿaṣ (Niphal: to take counsel, deliberate) appears in the imperfect Niphal indicating ongoing consultation. The elders who ‘stood before Solomon’ (haʿōmĕḏîm lipnê šĕlōmōh) use the liturgical standing-before vocabulary — ʿāmaḏ lipnê (to stand before) is the posture of attendant service. Their wisdom is tested wisdom; they have stood in the presence of the wisest king.
7: The elders advised: ‘If you will be kind (ṭôḇ) to this people and please them and speak good words (dĕḇārîm ṭôḇîm) to them, they will be your servants forever (laʿăḇādîm lĕḵā ḵol-hayyāmîm).’ ṭôḇ (good) as the predicate of wise kingship: goodness is the quality that secures permanent loyalty. dābār ṭôḇ (good word) is also the idiom for a favorable oracle or decree. The elders frame royal wisdom as covenantal speech-act: a good word spoken creates an enduring relationship.
8: But Rehoboam forsook (wayyaʿăzōḇ) the counsel (ʿēṣāh) of the elders. ʿāzaḇ (to forsake, abandon) is the apostasy verb: the same word used when Israel forsakes YHWH (2 Chronicles 12:1; 2 Chronicles 15:2; 2 Chronicles 24:20; Judges 2:12). The king’s political abandonment of the elders’ advice is linguistically identical to Israel’s theological abandonment of God. He consulted the young men (hayyĕlāḏîm) who had grown up with him. yĕlāḏîm = boys, lads (diminutive-connoted); the word choice demeans their wisdom before they speak.
9: ‘How do you advise me to answer this people?’ Rehoboam’s question to the young men is formally identical to his question to the elders (v6), but the context has already signaled the answer he wants. The young men’s response will mirror what he prefers rather than what is wise. The doubling of the consultation (elders / young men) is a literary device for moral contrast: wisdom rejected, folly chosen.
10: ‘My little finger (qaṭannî) shall be thicker than my father’s loins (motnê ʾāḇî).’ qaṭannî = my little one (from qāṭān, small); the young men’s advice uses a body-part boast. motnayim (loins, lower back/hips) is the seat of strength in Hebrew idiom. The comparison makes Rehoboam’s weakest point stronger than Solomon’s strongest: a claim of absolute surpassing that is transparently absurd and reveals the young men’s immaturity.
11: ‘My father burdened you with a heavy yoke (ʿōl kāḇēḏ), and I will add to your yoke (wĕʾănî ʾōsîp ʿal-ʿullĕkem). My father chastised you with whips (bĕšōṭîm), but I will chastise you with scorpions (bĕʿaqrabbîm).’ šōṭ (whip) vs. ʿaqraḇ (scorpion): the escalation suggests a spiked lash or multi-tailed whip (Byzantine scorpion whip) rather than a literal arachnid. The speech represents an extreme violation of the Deuteronomic royal ideal (Deuteronomy 17:14-20), which required humility, not domination.
12: Jeroboam and all the people came to Rehoboam on the third day as the king had said (kaʾăšer dibber hammelek lēmōr šûḇû ʾēlay bayyôm haššĕlîšî). The return on the third day fulfills the appointment and triggers the decision. The faithfulness of the people in returning is contrasted with the king’s faithlessness in his response. The parallel between ‘third day’ appointments kept and covenants broken is a recurring OT irony.
13: Rehoboam answered them harshly (qāšāh). qāšāh (to be hard/harsh, Hiphil: to make hard) is the hardening vocabulary: the same root as Pharaoh’s hardened heart in Exodus 7:3; 13:15 and Israel’s stiff-necked rebellion (Deuteronomy 9:6, qĕšēh-ʿōrep). Rehoboam’s harsh answer enacts the Egyptian paradigm: the new Pharaoh refusing to release his laborers. The Exodus pattern runs backward under Davidic apostasy.
14: He spoke to them according to the counsel of the young men (ʿăṣat hayyĕlāḏîm): ‘I will add to your yoke (ʾōsîp ʿal-ʿullĕkem) and I will chastise you with scorpions.’ The verbatim repetition of v11 confirms the choice. yāsap (Hiphil: to add) = to increase the burden. The Chronicler uses the same verb for the positive–—when YHWH adds to his people (2 Chronicles 25:9). Adding burden instead of blessing is the king’s inversion of the divine pattern.
15: ‘So the king did not listen to the people (wĕlōʾ-šāmaʿ hammelek ʾel-hāʿām), for the turn of events was from God (kî-hāyĕtāh nĕsibāh mēʾim hāʾĕlōhîm) that YHWH might establish his word.’ nĕsibāh (turn, turning point) is a rare noun (also 1 Kings 12:15) from sāḇaḇ (to turn, surround). The Chronicler inserts an explicit theological gloss: the political disaster is YHWH’s instrument to fulfill the prophetic word of Ahijah to Jeroboam (1 Kings 11:31). Divine sovereignty operates through human folly.
16: ‘What portion (ḥēleq) do we have in David? We have no inheritance (naḥalāh) in the son of Jesse!’ The schism cry uses covenant-land vocabulary: ḥēleq (portion, share in the land distribution) and naḥalāh (inheritance, the covenantal land right). The northern tribes declare themselves outside the Davidic covenant in the very terms of the land covenant. ‘To your tents, O Israel!’ (lĕʾōhāleḵā yiśrāʾēl) — the dispersal formula, used when assemblies conclude (1 Samuel 13:2; 2 Samuel 20:1). The northern kingdom’s founding act is a departure, not a founding assembly.
17: But Rehoboam reigned over the Israelites who were living in the cities of Judah. The Chronicler narrows the kingdom’s scope explicitly: Rehoboam now rules only a remnant of the whole, geographically limited to Judah. This contraction prepares the Chronicler’s theology that the true Israel is centered on Jerusalem and the Davidic dynasty — the northern kingdom is a political accident, not a legitimate covenant community.
18: King Rehoboam sent Hadoram (hăḏōrām) who was over the forced labor (ʿal-hammas). The people stoned him to death (wayyirgĕmû-ḇô ʾaḇen wayyāmōt). sāqal/rāgam (to stone) = the form of communal execution for grave offenses (Leviticus 24:16; Numbers 15:35). hammas (forced labor, corvée) is the taxation system that provoked the crisis; sending the official responsible for it first is either a miscalculation or a deliberate provocation. The stoning is the northern rejection of the entire administrative structure of Solomon’s kingdom.
19: ‘And Israel has been in rebellion (māraḏ) against the house of David to this day (ʿaḏ hayyôm hazzeh).’ māraḏ (to rebel) is the political-theological term for covenant disloyalty (Joshua 22:16–19; Numbers 14:9). ‘To this day’ (ʿaḏ hayyôm hazzeh) is the Chronicler’s closing editorial marker, noting that the division persists into the narrator’s own time. The northern rebellion is not merely historical: it is the ongoing condition of a community that has abandoned the Davidic covenant.
